title: 10 best herbal teas for gastroenteritis
intro: Ginger and chamomile teas are often recommended as soothing options for individuals with gastroenteritis, also known as the stomach flu. Ginger tea has anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce irritation in the stomach and alleviate symptoms like nausea and vomiting. Chamomile tea, on the other hand, possesses calming effects that can help relax the digestive muscles and reduce inflammation. Both teas can help rehydrate the body, which is crucial during bouts of gastroenteritis. However, it's important to note that while herbal teas can provide relief, they should not replace medical treatment. If symptoms persist or worsen, consult a healthcare professional.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea alleviates gastroenteritis through its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. This soothing herbal tea contains bisabolol and volatile oils, which relieve inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract and reduce muscle spasms. Additionally, its astringent qualities help minimize diarrhea. Furthermore, chamomile's drying effect on mucous membranes assists in eliminating excessive mucus in the intestines. Therefore, chamomile tea brings relief and healing to those suffering from gastroenteritis.
tea_parts: Flowers: The chamomile flowers contain anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that help alleviate inflammation and muscle spasms in the digestive tract, providing relief from gastroenteritis symptoms.
Leaves: Rich in calming compounds, chamomile leaves help soothe an upset stomach, reducing symptoms of gastroenteritis.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds are a source of antioxidants and essential oils that aid in digestion and help reduce inflammation, making them beneficial for gastroenteritis treatment.
tea_recipe: Measure - Boil 1 cup of water and let 1 chamomile tea bag steep for 5-10 minutes.
Squeeze - Once steeped, gently squeeze the tea bag to release any remaining chamomile extract.
Sweeten - Stir in 1 teaspoon of honey for added anti-inflammatory properties and soothe the throat (optional).
Dilute - If the tea is too strong, dilute it with an equal amount of hot water.
Serve - Pour the chamomile tea into a cup and enjoy while warm, up to three times a day.

teas.tea_desc: Peppermint tea brings relief to gastroenteritis sufferers due to its antispasmodic properties, which relax the gastrointestinal muscles, thereby alleviating painful spasms and cramps. This herbal tea's active compounds, particularly menthol, possess analgesic qualities that soothe and numb irritated gastrointestinal tract lining caused by the infection. Moreover, peppermint tea boasts carminative benefits, which dispel uncomfortable gas accumulation in the intestines, thus reducing bloating and promoting smoother digestion. Additionally, this tea's polyphenols exhibit antibacterial and antiviral properties, directly combating the pathogens responsible for gastroenteritis, consequently aiding in overall recovery. Lastly, peppermint tea's warmth and mild diuretic effect encourage increased fluid intake, preventing dehydration, a common concern with gastroenteritis, while simultaneously flushing out toxins.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in menthol and menthone, peppermint leaves promote digestion and soothe the stomach, making them ideal for treating gastroenteritis.
Flowers: High in antioxidants, peppermint flowers help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, providing relief from gastroenteritis symptoms.
Stems: Containing rosmarinic acid, peppermint stems have antispasmodic properties that help alleviate abdominal cramps and spasms caused by gastroenteritis.
Seeds: Packed with essential oils, peppermint seeds offer carminative effects, aiding in the expulsion of gas and easing discomfort due to gastroenteritis.
tea_recipe: Measure - Boil 8 ounces of water per cup of tea.
Crush - Gently crush 1 teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves per cup.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger tea provides relief from gastroenteritis due to its carminative and antiemetic properties, which mean it helps expel intestinal gas and alleviates nausea. The active compounds in ginger, such as gingerols and shogaols, possess anti-inflammatory qualities that soothe irritated stomach lining. Furthermore, ginger stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, promoting faster recovery. Additionally, its antimicrobial properties can combat the infection-causing bacteria or viruses in the gut, shortening the duration of gastroenteritis symptoms.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: Ginger's thick underground stem, or rhizome, contains powerful anti-inflammatory and antioxidant compounds, making it ideal for soothing digestive discomforts caused by gastroenteritis.
Leaves: Rich in mineral content and volatile oils, ginger leaves provide relief from gastrointestinal ailments, as they stimulate digestion and alleviate nausea.
Flowers: Ginger flowers contain medicinal properties that aid in treating various stomach disorders; they help reduce inflammation, cramping, and nausea associated with gastroenteritis.

Seeds: Ginger seeds hold essential oils and bioactive compounds, offering quick relief from gastrointestinal issues due to their carminative and antispasmodic effects, lessening abdominal pain and bloating.
tea_recipe: Peel and thinly slice 1 inch of fresh ginger root.
In a saucepan, combine ginger slices with 4 cups of water and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and let it simmer, covered, for 15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the ginger pieces.
Add honey or lemon to taste and drink while warm, up to three times a day.

teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea treats gastroenteritis by directly soothing the gastrointestinal mucosa due to its demulcent properties, thereby reducing inflammation in the stomach and intestines. Furthermore, its anti-inflammatory and anti-viral agents found in its active constituents, like glycyrrhizin, combat the infection causing the ailment. Additionally, licorice tea stimulates the production of mucus and fluids, providing a protective barrier against stomach acids. Lastly, it encourages the regeneration of damaged tissues in the gastrointestinal tract, resulting in the alleviation of gastroenteritis symptoms.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, which helps soothe inflamed tissues in the digestive tract, providing relief from gastroenteritis.
Rhizomes: Licorice rhizomes are rich in flavonoids that have an anti-inflammatory effect, making them ideal for easing symptoms of gastroenteritis.
Leaves: Licorice leaves contain various antioxidants and have been used to treat gastrointestinal issues, helping to alleviate the discomfort associated with gastroenteritis.
Flowers: The flowers of the licorice plant have compounds that help reduce spasms and have mild laxative properties useful for managing gastroenteritis.
Seeds: Licorice seeds contain essential oils that can stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, making it easier to treat gastroenteritis.
tea_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 tablespoon of licorice root, then boil for 10 minutes.
After boiling, remove the pot from heat and let it steep for 10 more minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the licorice root.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day.

teas.tea_desc: Slippery elm tea aids gastroenteritis due to its Demulcent properties, which involve the presence of mucilage. This mucilage turns into a slick gel when mixed with water, forming a protective layer in the digestive tract. This layer soothes inflammation, relieves pain, and encourages healing in the gastrointestinal tract's mucous membranes. Consequently, it eases symptoms of gastroenteritis, such as diarrhea, cramps, and nausea. The tea's antioxidant and immune-boosting qualities further bolster its therapeutic benefits, contributing to overall gut health and well-being.
tea_parts: Bark: The bark of the slippery elm tree is used to make medicinal tea for gastroenteritis due to its high mucilage content, which helps soothe inflamed tissues in the digestive tract.
Leaves: Slippery elm leaves are a source of antioxidants and have demulcent properties, making them useful for treating gastroenteritis symptoms.
Seeds: Slippery elm seeds contain mucilage that forms a soothing gel when mixed with water, making it helpful for treating gastrointestinal inflammation.
Roots: Slippery elm roots have a high mucilage content, which coats and protects irritated tissues in the digestive tract, providing relief from gastroenteritis symptoms.
Buds: Slippery elm buds contain beneficial compounds, including mucilage, which can help reduce inflammation and irritation in the digestive tract.
tea_recipe: In a cup, pour 8 ounces of boiling water over 1 tablespoon of dried slippery elm bark.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-10 minutes.
Stir the tea to help eliminate any thick residue.
Strain the tea to remove any bark particles.
Drink the tea while it is still warm, up to 3 times a day.

teas.tea_desc: Marshmallow tea, derived from the marshmallow plant's roots, contains a mucilaginous substance known as mucilage. This mucilage creates a soothing film over the mucous membranes in the digestive tract, reducing inflammation caused by gastroenteritis. Moreover, marshmallow tea's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties further alleviate irritation in the gut, offering symptomatic relief. Its demulcent qualities also aid in the protection of the gastrointestinal lining from acid and irritants, promoting healing. Lastly, marshmallow tea's hydrating effect helps maintain proper fluid balance in the body, combating dehydration resulting from gastroenteritis.
tea_parts: Roots: The marshmallow root contains mucilage, which soothes inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief from gastroenteritis.
Leaves: The leaves of the marshmallow plant are rich in mucilage and flavonoids, known to reduce inflammation and treat gastrointestinal symptoms.
Flowers: Marshmallow flowers have mucilaginous properties that help protect the mucous membranes of the digestive tract, making it ideal for treating gastroenteritis.
Seeds: Marshmallow seeds contain high levels of mucilage, which coats the digestive tract, easing discomfort and aiding in the healing process during gastroenteritis.
tea_recipe: In a small pot, combine 1 cup of water and 2.5 centimeters of fresh marshmallow root, then simmer for 15 minutes to release the active compounds.
Turn off the heat, add 1 teaspoon of chamomile flowers, and let the mixture steep for an additional 10 minutes to enhance the soothing properties.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup, discarding the solids.
Optionally, add honey to taste, stirring until fully dissolved, for added antimicrobial benefits and a touch of sweetness.
Sip the marshmallow tea slowly, allowing it to coat your digestive tract and help ease gastroenteritis symptoms.

teas.tea_desc: Fennel tea aids gastroenteritis by encouraging smooth muscle relaxation in the gastrointestinal tract, which alleviates abdominal cramps and facilitates the expulsion of gas. Its anti-inflammatory properties, derived from the volatile oil compound anethole, reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief from pain and discomfort. Fennel tea's antimicrobial and antispasmodic qualities make it an effective remedy for gastroenteritis symptoms, as it combats harmful bacteria and viruses while soothing intestinal spasms. Furthermore, fennel tea stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, enhancing nutrient absorption and promoting overall gastrointestinal health. Lastly, fennel tea's high levels of antioxidants counteract oxidative stress in the gastrointestinal tract, bolstering the immune system and accelerating recovery from gastroenteritis.
tea_parts: Roots: Fennel roots contain compounds with antispasmodic properties, providing relief from abdominal cramps associated with gastroenteritis.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of fennel are a rich source of anethole, a volatile oil that helps alleviate nausea and vomiting in gastroenteritis.
Leaves: Fennel leaves have carminative and anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the gastrointestinal tract, making them ideal for medicinal tea in gastroenteritis.
Seeds: Fennel seeds are renowned for their antimicrobial and antiflatulent properties that aid in combating infection and reducing bloating in gastroenteritis.
tea_recipe: Chop one tablespoon of fresh fennel seeds or one teaspoon of fennel seeds powder.
Place the chopped fennel in a teapot and add 2 cups of boiling water.
Let the mixture steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the solid particles.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day.

teas.tea_desc: Catnip tea contains nepetalactone, a compound that relaxes the intestinal muscles, thus aiding the passage of food and alleviating discomfort caused by gastroenteritis. This organic substance also boasts antispasmodic and carminative properties, which further soothe the digestive tract. Additionally, catnip tea offers anti-inflammatory benefits, helping reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal system. Consequently, the tea brings relief to those suffering from gastroenteritis, improving overall digestive health.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the catnip plant contain anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe inflammation in the digestive tract, providing relief from gastroenteritis symptoms.
Leaves: The leaves of the catnip plant are rich in calming compounds that help reduce muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract, alleviating discomfort caused by gastroenteritis.
Aerial Parts: (Note
Stems: Although less potent than roots and leaves, the stems of the catnip plant also contain volatile oils that can aid in digestion and help soothe an upset stomach.

Flowers: The flowers of the catnip plant, while less commonly used, can be included in teas to provide a subtle, pleasant taste and contribute to the calming effects beneficial for gastroenteritis symptoms.
tea_recipe: Steep 1 teaspoon of dried catnip leaves in 1 cup of boiling water for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, leaving only the liquid.
Let the tea cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Drink 3 cups of this catnip tea per day, spread out throughout the day.
Continue this treatment for 3-5 days or until symptoms subside.

teas.tea_desc: Dandelion tea assists with gastroenteritis due to its anti-inflammatory properties, derived from the plant's sesquiterpene lactone content. This natural tea promotes digestion by stimulating the production of bile in the liver and gastric juices in the stomach. Furthermore, dandelion tea boasts antioxidant qualities, containing phenolic acids and flavonoids, which protect the gastrointestinal tract from oxidative stress and damage. Additionally, this herbal tea offers diuretic benefits, flushing out toxins and helping maintain proper fluid balance in the body, thus alleviating gastroenteritis symptoms. Lastly, dandelion tea's demulcent properties soothe the gastrointestinal lining, reducing irritation and providing relief from gastroenteritis-related discomfort.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of dandelions are rich in nutrients and possess anti-inflammatory properties that can alleviate gastroenteritis symptoms.
Leaves: Dandelion leaves are known to stimulate digestion and contain compounds that help in soothing an irritated gastrointestinal tract.
Flowers: The bright yellow flowers of dandelions have diuretic effects that aid in eliminating toxins from the body, supporting gastroenteritis recovery.
tea_recipe: Gather 2-3 dandelion heads and rinse them thoroughly under running water to remove dirt and bugs.
Separate the bright yellow petals from the green base and place them in a tea infuser or reusable tea bag.
Boil 1 cup of water and let it cool for 2-3 minutes before pouring it over the dandelion petals.
Steep the petals in hot water for 5-10 minutes, then discard the used petals.
Drink the tea up to three times a day for gastroenteritis relief.

teas.tea_desc: Skullcap tea contains antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties that directly address two prominent symptoms of gastroenteritis - nausea and inflammation. This herbal tea's antispasmodic qualities alleviate abdominal pain and muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract caused by the infection. Furthermore, skullcap tea's astringent properties help manage diarrhea by gently constricting the tissues in the intestines, reducing the frequency and volume of bowel movements. Ultimately, skullcap tea's comprehensive action against gastroenteritis symptoms makes it an ideal natural remedy for this ailment.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in tannins and volatile oils, the leaves of skullcap ease stomach inflammation and help control diarrhea.
Flowers: Packed with antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, skullcap flowers help soothe an upset stomach and alleviate symptoms of gastroenteritis.
Stems: Containing anti-spasmodic properties, the stems of skullcap assist in regulating intestinal muscle contractions and reducing abdominal cramping.

Aerial Parts: While not a specific part from the list, aerial parts (above-ground portions) of skullcap, often used interchangeably with the above parts, is known to provide overall relief for gastroenteritis symptoms due to its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic properties.
tea_recipe: In a cup, pour boiling water over 1-2 teaspoons of dried skullcap herb.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the liquid to remove the herb.
Optional: Add honey or lemon for flavor.
Drink the tea 3 times a day, between meals, to soothe gastroenteritis symptoms.
